metadata.analyzer_model: claude-sonnet-5 (graded inline in the main session, not a separate analyzer subagent)

notes: Only 1 run per configuration was taken (no repeated sampling) — stddev/min/max across evals reflect variance between the 3 different test cases, not repeated noise on the same prompt. Treat this as a first pass, not a statistically confident benchmark.

The skill's biggest wins are on the two workflow evals (basic-update-request, team-wide-scope-inference): without the skill, Claude never asks about update scope and never mentions verifying with `claude plugin list`.

On no-update-available-troubleshooting, with_skill and without_skill tie at 100% — this eval mostly exercises general troubleshooting + tool use (checking plugin.json/git state), which the baseline can do equally well without any skill-specific knowledge. This assertion set doesn't strongly discriminate for that eval; consider adding an assertion that's specific to the skill's exact troubleshooting section wording if a stronger signal is wanted.

team-wide-scope-inference's without_skill run took 246s (vs 22-74s everywhere else) because it went and inspected the actual repo/git state in depth. That depth is exactly why it caught a real gap in the skill (see next note) — the extra time bought something real here, but this is a single data point and could also just be a slow outlier.

Important finding, not visible in the raw pass rate: on team-wide-scope-inference, the without_skill run correctly identified that `--scope project` only pins config in git — it does not push the update to teammates' machines, who each still need to pull and run their own `claude plugin marketplace update`. The with_skill run passed its assertions but never surfaced this distinction. The skill should be revised to explain what project scope does and doesn't do for teammates, not just how to set it.
